---
title: "install-nfiesta"
format: html
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Part 1 — Mirror the core GitLab repos into your personal GitHub

The core repos are:

-   nfiesta_pg — main estimation engine.
    [\[LINK\]](https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_pg?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

-   nfiesta_htc — Horvitz–Thompson continuous-population extension.
    [\[LINK\]](https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_htc?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

-   nfiesta_sdesign — sampling-design extension.
    [\[LINK\]](https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_sdesign?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

GitLab wikis are separate Git repositories, and GitLab documents that
you can clone them from the Wiki area using Clone repository.

## Step 1. Create empty repos on GitHub

Create these empty GitHub repositories in your account:

nfiesta_pg

-   nfiesta_htc

-   nfiesta_sdesign

Do not initialize them with a README.

## Step 2. Mirror nfiesta_pg

In Terminal:

``` bash
mkdir -p ~/code/nfiesta 
cd ~/code/nfiesta

git clone https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_pg.git 
cd nfiesta_pg

git remote rename origin upstream 
git remote add origin https://github.com/gaelso/nfiesta_pg.git

git push -u origin --all 
git push -u origin --tags
```

This gives you:

-   upstream = original GitLab repo

-   origin = your GitHub mirror

## Step 3. Mirror nfiesta_htc

``` bash
cd ~/code/nfiesta 
git clone https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_htc.git 
cd nfiesta_htc

git remote rename origin upstream 
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/nfiesta_htc.git

git push -u origin --all 
git push -u origin --tags
```

## Step 4. Mirror nfiesta_sdesign

``` bash
cd ~/code/nfiesta 
git clone https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_sdesign.git 
cd nfiesta_sdesign

git remote rename origin upstream 
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/nfiesta_sdesign.git

git push -u origin --all 
git push -u origin --tags
```

## Step 5. Also mirror the wiki for nfiesta_pg

This matters because the README points to the wiki for installation and
the minimal example. GitLab documents that project wikis are separate
repos.

``` bash
cd ~/code/nfiesta 
git clone https://gitlab.com/nfiesta/nfiesta_pg.wiki.git 
cd nfiesta_pg.wiki
```

Now create a GitHub repo named something like nfiesta_pg_wiki, then:

``` bash
git remote rename origin upstream 
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_GITHUB_USERNAME/nfiesta_pg_wiki.git

git push -u origin --all
```

## Step 6. Verify remotes

For each repo:

``` bash

git remote -v
```

You want to see both origin and upstream.

## Step 7. Update your mirrors later

When the GitLab upstream changes:

``` bash
git fetch upstream 
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main 
git push origin main
```

If the default branch is master instead of main, use master.

# Part 2 - Install nFiesta in a docker container

NOTE for macOS:

-   Postgres installation via homebrew doesn't ship with python support.

-   Postgres installation via Postgres.app doesn't allow modification of
    the DB (ex. `make install` ) as modification to the Application
    folder aare forbiden even with sudo).

-   The safest path is to run everything inside a docker container.

> PostgreSQL extensions need their control files, SQL scripts, and
> sometimes shared libraries installed into the server’s extension
> directories before CREATE EXTENSION will work. PostgreSQL’s extension
> docs and PGXS build docs describe exactly that model, which is why a
> writable Linux container is a good fit here.

## Final folder structure

Use one project root:

```         
~/code/nfiesta/
├── docker/
│   ├── Dockerfile
│   └── docker-compose.yml
├── nfiesta_pg/
├── nfiesta_htc/
└── nfiesta_sdesign/
```

This keeps:

-   `nfiesta_*` repos as upstream mirrors,

-   `docker/` as your local infrastructure,

-   one clean mount point into the container.

That structure is just a practical project convention, but it lines up
well with Docker’s normal “build context” model and with PostgreSQL
extension development using PGXS.

## Step 1 — Create the project folders

``` bash
mkdir -p ~/code/nfiesta/docker
mkdir -p ~/code/nfiesta
cd ~/code/nfiesta
```

If your repos already exist somewhere else, move them into
`~/code/nfiesta/` so you end up with:

``` bash
ls ~/code/nfiesta
# docker  nfiesta_pg  nfiesta_htc  nfiesta_sdesign
```

## Step 2 — Put the Docker files in `~/code/nfiesta/docker`

Go there:

``` bash
cd ~/code/nfiesta/docker
```

Create the files:

``` bash
touch Dockerfile docker-compose.yml
```

## Step 3 — Create the Dockerfile

Open `~/code/nfiesta/docker/Dockerfile` and paste this:

``` dockerfile
FROM postgres:16

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    postgresql-server-dev-16 \
    postgresql-plpython3-16 \
    postgresql-16-postgis-3 \
    build-essential \
    git \
    python3 \
    python3-pip \
    python3-numpy \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
```

Why this image and these packages:

-   `postgres:16` is the official PostgreSQL Docker image.

-   `postgresql-server-dev-16` is what PGXS-based extension builds
    normally need.

-   `postgresql-plpython3-16` provides `plpython3u`, which you need for
    nFIESTA. PostgreSQL documents `plpython3u` as the extension name to
    load.

-   `postgresql-16-postgis-3` provides PostGIS for PostgreSQL 16.
    PostGIS is an extension to PostgreSQL and requires PostgreSQL
    server-side installation support.

## Step 4 — Create `docker-compose.yml`

Open `~/code/nfiesta/docker/docker-compose.yml` and paste this:

``` yaml
services:
  db:
    build: .
    container_name: nfiesta-db
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: postgres
    ports:
      - "5433:5432"
    volumes:
      - ../:/src
      - nfiesta_pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    working_dir: /src

volumes:
  nfiesta_pgdata:
```

Why port `5433`:

-   it avoids conflicts with Homebrew PostgreSQL or Postgres.app if
    either is still installed or accidentally running. The official
    Postgres image listens on `5432` inside the container; you can map
    that to any free host port.

Why `../:/src`:

-   from inside `~/code/nfiesta/docker`, `../` is your whole
    `~/code/nfiesta` project root,

-   so inside the container you will see:

    -   `/src/nfiesta_pg`

    -   `/src/nfiesta_htc`

    -   `/src/nfiesta_sdesign`

    -   `/src/docker`

## Step 5 — Build and start the container

From `~/code/nfiesta/docker`:

``` bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

This will:

-   build the custom image,

-   create the `nfiesta-db` container,

-   start PostgreSQL,

-   persist your database in the named volume `nfiesta_pgdata`.

Check that it is running:

``` bash
docker compose ps
```

## Step 6 — Enter the container

``` bash
docker exec -it nfiesta-db bash
```

Inside the container, verify the repos are mounted:

``` bash
ls /src
```

You should see:

``` text
docker
nfiesta_pg
nfiesta_htc
nfiesta_sdesign
```

## Step 7 — Verify PostgreSQL, PostGIS, and PL/Python

Still inside the container:

``` bash
psql -U postgres -d postgres
```

Then run:

``` sql
CREATE EXTENSION postgis;
CREATE EXTENSION plpython3u;
```

PostgreSQL’s docs say `CREATE EXTENSION` loads the installed extension
into the current database, and that the extension must already be
installed on the system.

Optional checks:

``` sql
SELECT PostGIS_Full_Version();
SELECT name FROM pg_available_extensions WHERE name IN ('postgis', 'plpython3u');
```

PostGIS recommends checking installed versions with SQL functions like
`postgis_full_version()`.

Exit `psql` with:

``` sql
\q
```

## Step 8 — Build and install `nfiesta_htc`

Inside the container:

``` bash
cd /src/nfiesta_htc
make
make install
```

Why this should work here:

-   PGXS is designed for building PostgreSQL extensions against an
    installed server,

-   and in the container the PostgreSQL extension directories are
    writable.

If `make` fails, run:

``` bash
pg_config --version
pg_config --sharedir
pg_config --pkglibdir
```

That tells you exactly which PostgreSQL installation the build is
targeting. PGXS relies on `pg_config` for that build/install path
information.

## Step 9 — Build and install `nfiesta_sdesign`

``` bash
cd /src/nfiesta_sdesign
make
make install
```

NOTE: If `make` triggers an error, it is because it runs a test:

`make installcheck REGRESS_OPTS="--dbname=contrib_regression_sdesign"`

which will not work without data. It can still installed, so skip `make`
and proceed directly to `make install`.

## Step 10 — Build and install `nfiesta_pg`

``` bash
cd /src/nfiesta_pg
make
make install
```

At this point, the extension control files and SQL scripts should be
installed into the PostgreSQL 16 directories inside the container, which
is the prerequisite for `CREATE EXTENSION`.

## Step 11 — Create your working database

Open `psql` again:

``` bash
psql -U postgres -d postgres
```

Create the database:

``` sql
CREATE DATABASE nfiesta_dev;
```

Connect to it:

``` sql
\c nfiesta_dev
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 12 — Enable the extensions in `nfiesta_dev`

Run:

``` sql
CREATE EXTENSION postgis;
CREATE EXTENSION plpython3u;
CREATE EXTENSION htc;
CREATE EXTENSION nfiesta_sdesign;
CREATE EXTENSION nfiesta_pg;
```

If one of the nFIESTA extension names differs from the repo name,
inspect available extensions:

```         
SELECT name FROM pg_available_extensions
WHERE name IN ('htc', 'nfiesta_sdesign', 'nfiesta_pg');
```

`CREATE EXTENSION` only works after the extension files are installed
and the control file is present in PostgreSQL’s extension path.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 13 — Test from your Mac

From your Mac terminal, not inside the container:

```         
psql -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5433 -U postgres -d nfiesta_dev
```

Password:

```         
postgres
```

This works because `docker-compose.yml` mapped host port `5433` to
container port `5432`. The official Postgres container is designed to
accept network connections this way.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 14 — Connect from R

In R:

```         
install.packages(c("DBI", "RPostgres"))

library(DBI)

con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "nfiesta_dev",
  host = "127.0.0.1",
  port = 5433,
  user = "postgres",
  password = "postgres"
)

dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT current_database(), current_user;")
```

That gives you the architecture you wanted:

-   

-   PostgreSQL for storage,

-   

-   R for processing,

-   

-   nFIESTA extensions installed in a controlled server.

-   

The database side is just standard PostgreSQL network access, so R does
not care that the server is in Docker. The key PostgreSQL requirement is
simply that the server be reachable and the extensions be installed in
that server instance.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 15 — Stop and restart later

From `~/code/nfiesta/docker`:

```         
docker compose stop
docker compose start
```

To rebuild after changing the Dockerfile:

```         
docker compose up -d --build
```

To remove the container but keep database data:

```         
docker compose down
```

To remove everything including the database volume:

```         
docker compose down -v
```

Named volumes persist PostgreSQL data across container restarts unless
you explicitly remove them. That is standard Docker volume behavior for
stateful services like PostgreSQL.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Recommended cleanup on your Mac

For now:

-   

-   stop Homebrew PostgreSQL,

-   

-   stop Postgres.app,

-   

-   keep them installed until Docker is working,

-   

-   then uninstall the ones you do not want.

-   

That avoids port conflicts and “wrong `pg_config` / wrong `psql`”
confusion. PostgreSQL extension builds are sensitive to which server
installation `pg_config` points to.

Use:

```         
brew services stop postgresql@16
```

And make sure Postgres.app is not running.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The one thing to check before building nFIESTA

Make sure your repos are actually here:

```         
~/code/nfiesta/nfiesta_pg
~/code/nfiesta/nfiesta_htc
~/code/nfiesta/nfiesta_sdesign
```

If they are not, the volume mount will work, but the `cd /src/...` steps
will fail.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## If you want the shortest possible path

Create these two files in `~/code/nfiesta/docker`, run:

```         
docker compose up -d --build
docker exec -it nfiesta-db bash
```

and then inside the container:

```         
psql -U postgres -d postgres -c "CREATE EXTENSION postgis;"
psql -U postgres -d postgres -c "CREATE EXTENSION plpython3u;"
cd /src/nfiesta_htc && make && make install
cd /src/nfiesta_sdesign && make && make install
cd /src/nfiesta_pg && make && make install
psql -U postgres -d postgres -c "CREATE DATABASE nfiesta_dev;"
psql -U postgres -d nfiesta_dev -c "CREATE EXTENSION postgis;"
psql -U postgres -d nfiesta_dev -c "CREATE EXTENSION plpython3u;"
psql -U postgres -d nfiesta_dev -c "CREATE EXTENSION htc;"
psql -U postgres -d nfiesta_dev -c "CREATE EXTENSION nfiesta_sdesign;"
psql -U postgres -d nfiesta_dev -c "CREATE EXTENSION nfiesta_pg;"
```

If you hit an error at any step, paste the exact command and full output
and I’ll debug that specific point.
